original_abstract: Spatial transcriptomics technologies provide critical gene expression profiles within their original spatial context, necessitating computational tools capable of effectively handling spatial clustering, multisample integration, and cell-type deconvolution. We present GraphST, a graph self-supervised contrastive learning framework designed to maximize the information extracted from spatial transcriptomics data. By integrating graph neural networks with self-supervised learning, GraphST generates highly informative and discriminative spot representations by minimizing the embedding distances between spatially adjacent neighbors. Through extensive benchmarking across diverse tissue types and diverse technology platforms, we demonstrate that GraphST consistently outperforms existing methods. Locally, GraphST achieved a 10% improvement in clustering accuracy while successfully delineating fine-grained anatomical structures in brain and embryo tissues. Furthermore, GraphST serves as a versatile tool for multisample analysis, uniquely supporting both vertical and horizontal integration while effectively correcting for batch effects. Finally, GraphST exhibits superior performance in cell-type deconvolution, accurately capturing complex spatial niches such as germinal centers in lymph nodes and the localization of exhausted tumor-infiltrating T cells in breast cancer.
humanized_abstract: Current spatial transcriptomics technologies offer gene expression information at the right place and at the time, which demands efficient analysis for clustering in the spatial context, multi-sample integration and cell type deconvolution. In this work, we proposed a graph self-supervised contrastive learning framework GraphST to fully explore the underlying information of single-cell spatial transcriptomics data. GraphST integrates graph neural networks and self-supervised learning to produce powerful and discriminative features for ST data by minimizing the distance of neighboring points in the embedding space in the spatial context. Through experiments on various types of tissues and ST technologies, we showed that GraphST is generally better than state-of-the-art methods. In terms of local analysis, GraphST achieves a 10% improvement in clustering accuracy and reveals finer anatomical details in mouse brain and embryo samples. As for multi-sample analysis, GraphST supports both vertical and horizontal integration and can effectively remove batch effects. GraphST also has a better performance in cell type deconvolution, which can capture the intricate spatial niches such as germinal centers in lymph nodes and exhausted tumor-infiltrating T cells in breast cancer.
